name: RawData+opencog-ml+contributing to Opencog (unsupervised language learning)
content:
Source: opencog-dev mailing list
Thread: contributing to Opencog (unsupervised language learning)
Date range: Mon, 1 Feb 2016 11:01:55 -0800 (PST) to Sat, 13 Feb 2016 02:30:27 -0800 (PST)
Messages: 13
Participants: Roland Coeurjoly, Linas Vepstas, Rohit Shinde, Stefano Bielli, Ben Goertzel
---

From: Roland Coeurjoly
Date: Mon, 1 Feb 2016 11:01:55 -0800 (PST)

Hi all,

My name is Roland and I would like to contribute to the unsupervised 
language learning part of the OpenCog project.
I have built Opencog and read the arxiv paper. I have also read chunks of 
the EGI volumes and the relevant wikis for language learning.

What language [...]

---

From: Linas Vepstas
Date: Mon, 1 Feb 2016 20:06:21 -0600

Hi Roland,

Yes, the learning curve is steep, but the path is relatively
straight-forward. Basically, one configures opencog, crunches a bunch of
data, and soon thereafter, arrive at a spot where daata analysis and
research is needed; at this point, it becomes a mixture of thinking about
theory and writing code to implement experiments and extend the processing
pipeline to the next stage (assuming the experiments look good).

Can I guess that you speak French? There are two related tasks: for
languages such as French, there is a need to make sure we can correctly
identify stems and suffixes. i.e. do some rather basic morphology.   For
English, we can ignore morphology, and move straight to trying to deduce
syntax. The wiki and the arxiv explains some of this, and I have various
PDF's that [...]

---

From: Roland Coeurjoly
Date: Tue, 2 Feb 2016 06:40:29 +0100

Yes, I speak French, Spanish, English and I can defend myself in Mandarin
Chinese.
3 years ago I became fascinated with language acquisition (the way humans
do it), so I have read many articles and books about the subject.
I have done a fair bit of programming in C++ and I am [...]

---

From: Rohit Shinde
Date: Tue, 2 Feb 2016 11:37:17 +0530

Hi Roland,

I participated in GSoC last year under the mentorship of Linas. I worked on
the Unsupervised Language Learning project. It is fantastic that you know
so many languages.

You need to clone the atomspace, opencog, and cogutils and install them
first. Then you can setup [...]

---

From: Linas Vepstas
Date: Tue, 2 Feb 2016 14:09:36 -0600

Excellent!

If you have not read the original link-grammar papers, you should do so
now. I don't think they're very hard to understand -- what you need to know
is the key insight for syntax and parsing: that words behave like jigsaw
puzzle pieces (aka "disjuncts"), and that parsing requires picking out the
correct disjuncts, so that they "snap together" to form a sentence. (As it
happens, this is equivalent to other, very different-seeming theories of
syntax.  If you know other theories, not to worry, they're all essentially
identical.)

The key insight into learning is that these disjuncts can be inferred as
statistical correlations: they correspond to word arrangements that are
more likely than 'random'.  These can be picked out by pair-wise
correlations, using high mutual information [...]

Linas, Rohit,

From now on I am going to do 3 things:

-Ask for help if I get stuck. I will also search this mailing list for
answers (notably the conversations between you [...]

Regarding the theory, I've been reading the link grammar paper and your

I guess it is going to take some time until I can [...]

From: Linas Vepstas
Date: Thu, 4 Feb 2016 13:52:46 -0600

Hi Roland,

On Thu, Feb 4, 2016 at 1:40 PM, Roland Coeurjoly <rolancl91@gmail.com>
wrote:

yes.

That is completely unrelated. It uses an entirely different technology,
different approach, solves a different problem.

The approach you do want to follow is here:
http://wiki.opencog.org/w/Language_learning

There are several ways to contribute.

-- nothing can be done practically, without a database of of statistics.
Rohit has such a database.  One step would be to figure out how to have him
share it with you.  The other step is to generate your own copy from
scratch, but that takes work and a few CPU-months of compute time.

-- nothing can be done until you have a very clear idea of what the next
step is, and hold that in mind as you proceed.  The point of studying the
theory is to be able [...]

---

From: Linas Vepstas
Date: Fri, 5 Feb 2016 17:28:26 -0600

Hi Rich,

(Hi Roland, you are mentioned below)

On Tue, Feb 2, 2016 at 5:17 PM, Rich Cooper <Rich@englishlogickernel.com>
wrote:

Thank you, yes, exactly  (Rich, btw, sorry, your post was held for 3 days
as spam! some spam filter does not like you or what you said.)

Yuret's thesis is a central, important foundation of the link-grammar
language-learning project.  Basically, the very first step of the project
is to replicate Yuret's work.  This is not hard, because all the scripts
have been written, all the code exists, it all works fine and has been
tested repeatedly; Most recently Rohit has built up a database containing
such data.

The second step is to take Yuret-style data, and automatically create LG
disjuncts for each word.  This code has also been written; I think Rohit
wrote it or [...]

From: Ben Goertzel
Date: Thu, 11 Feb 2016 20:34:44 +0800

Fantastic -- welcome, Stefano !

On Thu, Feb 11, 2016 at 6:02 PM, Stefano Bielli
<stefano.bielli@gmail.com> wrote:

---

From: Linas Vepstas
Date: Fri, 12 Feb 2016 22:08:27 -0600

Hi Stefano!

I think that would be wonderful. This is the sort of project where mostly,
one has to just dive right in. I'm not sure what to suggest. Let me know if
you have questions.

Oh, I do know what to suggest: read at least the first half of the first
few link-grammar papers, they describe various basic things you must know.
Read also, if you can, Deniz Yuret's thesis, it is another cornerstone.

--linas

On Thu, Feb 11, 2016 at 4:02 AM, Stefano Bielli <stefano.bielli@gmail.com>
wrote:
